coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) exhibit distinctive optical, electronic, and physicochemical properties, rendering them effective cocatalysts to enhance the photocatalytic performance of light-absorbing materials. The interplay between CDs and substrates is pivotal in manipulating photogenerated charge separation, transfer, and redistribution, significantly influencing overall photocatalytic efficiency. This study introduces a novel electrostatic interaction strategy to interface positively charged CdS nanorods (CdS NRs) with negatively charged furfural-derived CDs. The resulting optimized composite (25-CDs@CdS NRs), showcases photocatalytic hydrogen production at a rate of 1076 μmol g−1h−1. Experimental analyses and theoretical simulations offer insights into the structure–activity relationship, underscoring the crucial role of enhanced electrostatic interaction between CDs and CdS NRs in facilitating efficient charge transfer, activating reaction sites, and improving reaction kinetics. This research establishes an adaptable strategy for integrating CDs with metal-based semiconductors, opening new avenues for developing photocatalytic hybrid assemblies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Schematic illustration for the synthesis of CDs@CdS NRs. B) SEM image of 25-CDs@CdS NRs. C-E) TEM and HR-TEM images of 25-CDs@CdS NRs; F) HR-TEM image of CDs; G) High-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image of 25-CDs@CdS NRs and H-K) corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) elemental mappings images for S, Cd, O, and C.

A) Raman spectra of CdS NRs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs (Excitation wavelength = 633 nm); B) Raman spectra of CDs powder (Excitation wavelength = 633 nm); C) Zeta potential distribution of CdS NRs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs aqueous solution; D) Zeta potential distribution of furfural and CDs aqueous solution; E) High-resolution XPS spectra of Cd 3d of CdS NRs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs; F) High-resolution XPS spectra of S 2p of CdS NRs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs.

A, B) Visible-light-driven H2 production by CDs@CdS NRs with different CDs contents; C, D) H2 production by physically-mixed CdS NRs with different amounts of CDs (CDs/CdS NRs); E) H2 production by physically-mixed CdS NRs with different amounts of furfural (F/CdS NRs); F) Cycling test of the photocatalytic H2 evolution by 25-CDs@CdS NRs under visible light irradiation (The number of repeat experiments: n = 2).

A) The UV–vis spectra of the CdS NRs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs composites (inset: the photograph of CdS NRs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs powder under daylight); The corresponding Tauc plots of B) 25-CDs@CdS NR and C) CdS NRs.

A) The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs, CdS NRs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs powders (Excitation wavelength = 320 nm); B) PL decay spectra of CdS NRs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs; C) Photocurrent density (300 W Xenon lamp, 100 mW/cm2; Electrolyte: 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution); D) Linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) spectra (Electrolyte: 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution); and E) Incident photo-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra of CDs, CdS NRs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs; F) Illustrative mechanistic diagram of photocatalytic H2 evolution over 25-CDs@CdS NRs under visible light.

A, B) Top and side views of the computational model with CDs on CdS (001) surface. All possible HER active sites around CDs are marked. Color map: purple—Cd; green—S; black—C. (C) Free energy diagram of proton adsorption on all marked sites. The value of a commercial Pt|C catalyst is also plotted for reference. D) Charge density difference plot of the CDs-attached CdS (001) surface. The isosurface value is set as 0.002e/Å3 and yellow indicates charge accumulation while cyan represents charge depletion. Most of these surrounding sites possess considerable HER activity according to our calculation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of the H2 evolution rate of 25-CDs@CdS NRs with other recent works involving CDs and CdS photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Electrostatically induced Furfural-Derived carbon Dots-CdS hybrid for solar Light-Driven hydrogen production

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) exhibit distinctive optical, electronic, and physicochemical properties, rendering them effective cocatalysts to enhance the photocatalytic performance of light-absorbing materials. The interplay between CDs and substrates is pivotal in manipulating photogenerated charge separation, transfer, and redistribution, significantly influencing overall photocatalytic efficiency. This study introduces a novel electrostatic interaction strategy to interface positively charged CdS nanorods (CdS NRs) with negatively charged furfural-derived CDs. The resulting optimized composite (25-CDs@CdS NRs), showcases photocatalytic hydrogen production at a rate of 1076 μmol g−1h−1. Experimental analyses and theoretical simulations offer insights into the structure–activity relationship, underscoring the crucial role of enhanced electrostatic interaction between CDs and CdS NRs in facilitating efficient charge transfer, activating reaction sites, and improving reaction kinetics. This research establishes an adaptable strategy for integrating CDs with metal-based semiconductors, opening new avenues for developing photocatalytic hybrid assemblies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Artificial photosynthesis stands out as an enticing technique for converting solar energy into chemicals and clean fuels [1–3]. Solar-driven water splitting, a crucial facet of artificial photosynthesis aimed at hydrogen production, is regarded as a sustainable avenue for constructing a carbon-free energy economy to address the global energy crisis [4–7]. Various semiconductor materials (e.g., TiO2, ZnO, SnO2, and CdS), exhibiting suitable band structures and light-harvesting capabilities, have been devised for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [8–12]. Among these, chalcogenide cadmium sulfide (CdS) emerges as a promising photocatalyst, boasting excellent optical and electrical properties and a relatively narrow band gap (2.40 eV) conducive to visible light absorption [13,14]. This positions CdS ideally for visible-light-driven hydrogen evolution. However, the performance of CdS is hampered by photogenerated holes-induced photo-corrosion and rapid charge-carrier recombination [15]. To overcome these limitations, approaches such as heterojunction construction [16] and surface modification through the immobilization of metal-based nanoparticles, such as Au [17] and Pt [18], onto CdS have been demonstrated as effective strategies to enhance catalytic efficiency [19,20]. Nevertheless, the use of expensive and scarce noble metals imposes constraints on the scalability of these applications, necessitating the development of affordable alternatives [21–23].

As a burgeoning class of fluorescent metal-free nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) are gaining recognition for their prowess as excellent photocatalysts [24]. This acknowledgment stems from their distinctive optical properties, adjustable functional surface moieties, unique electron reservoirs, and high specific surface area [25–29]. Biomass-derived carbon dots, sourced from renewable biomass materials such as corn, starch, enokitake mushrooms, various fruits, rice husks, chitosan, alkali lignin, and willow catkins, present numerous advantages across diverse applications [30–37]. However, it is crucial to acknowledge the inherent limitations of current methods for producing carbon dots using these biomass sources, including scalability, cost, purification, and tunability. The use of edible materials for carbon dot synthesis, given the global issue of food scarcity, is impractical and wasteful. Consequently, there is a growing emphasis on utilizing agricultural residues or plant waste to align with environmentally friendly and sustainable practices. Furfural, a pivotal chemical derived from cellulose, holds significant promise and has been identified by the U.S. Department of Energy as a critical platform compound with substantial development potential [38]. It is noteworthy that furfural, as the raw material for carbon dot synthesis, possesses a higher level of purity. This heightened purity ensures stable quality and performance across different batches. In comparison to other biomass resources, furfural provides a more explicit molecular formula, ensuring consistency in the quality of carbon dots. This advantage not only contributes to enhancing carbon dot performance but also addresses potential batch-to-batch instability issues that may arise when using other biomass resources. The integration of CdS with biomass-derived CDs to create heterogeneous catalysts [22,39,40] holds the potential to enhance photocatalytic activity by improving light absorbance and stability, providing active sites, and facilitating charge carrier separation and transfer [41–44]. However, most reported methods for CDs preparation involve calcination and secondary carbonization [40,45], and the integration between CDs and CdS typically relies on a physical combination with relatively weak interfacial interactions. Against this backdrop, there is an urgent need to develop facile and effective strategies for CDs synthesis and achieve close contact and strong interactions between CDs and CdS.

In our study, we introduce a novel approach that exploits the distinctive properties of furfural to augment the performance of photocatalysts. One notable characteristic of furfural is its electronegative nature in aqueous solutions. Building upon this property, we have devised a groundbreaking strategy that capitalizes on electrostatic interactions. Specifically, we leverage the negative charge of furfural-derived carbon dots (CDs) to effectively couple them with positively charged CdS nanorods (CdS NRs). This innovative approach significantly enhances the overall photocatalytic performance. The experimental results vividly illustrate the effective interaction between CDs and CdS, leading to improved light absorption and charge separation. Theoretical simulations corroborate this observation, indicating that the modification with CDs activates inert S atoms in CdS, thereby enhancing proton adsorption, charge redistribution, and hydrogen production kinetics. Consequently, the optimized 25-CDs@CdS NRs exhibit a hydrogen production rate of 1076 μmol g−1h−1 under visible light irradiation, surpassing that of CdS NRs by fivefold. Through this work, we aim to bring to the forefront the immense potential of furfural and its capacity to improve the performance of hybrid photocatalysts significantly.

Cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate ([Cd (NO3)2·4H2O], 99.99 % metals basis) and furfural (AR, 99 %) were purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Thiourea ((NH2)2CS, AR, 99 %) was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). Anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na2SO3, AR) and anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, AR) were obtained from Tianjin Dongli Tianda Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O, AR, ≥98.0 %) was purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (C2H5OH) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents were used as received without further purification. Deionized water was generated using a Smart-Q15 UT ultrapure water system (Hetai Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+ (USA) with a monochromatic Al Ka X-ray source. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken with a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Japan). Scanning transmission electron microscopy and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (STEM-EDS) elemental mappings were carried out with an FEI TalosF200x and Super-X EDS (Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using an X'Pert3 Powder X-ray diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical Ltd., Malvern, UK). Raman spectra were recorded using a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution (Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a TU-1950 UV–vis spectrofluorometer (Persee General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Photoluminescence (PL) was measured using an LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer Co., Ltd, USA). Fluorescence decay curves of samples were recorded using an FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Livingston, UK). Zeta potential spectra were recorded using a Zeta potentiometer ZS-90 (Malvern Instruments Co., Ltd., UK).

Cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (20 mmol) and 60 mmol thiourea were dissolved into 60 mL of ethylenediamine solution under vigorous stirring for 60 min. The resulting mixture was subsequently transferred to a 100 mL autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 48 h. After allowing it to naturally cool, the bright yellow powders were subjected to three cycles of washing with deionized water and ethanol alternately, followed by collection through centrifugation. Subsequently, the precipitates were dried at 60 °C in an oven to yield the final samples.

CDs@CdS NRs were synthesized using a straightforward hydrothermal method. In a beaker, CdS NRs (150 mg), and furfural solutions at various volumes (5, 25, 55, 85 μL) were thoroughly mixed with 50 mL of deionized water, and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, the blended solution was transferred to a 100 mL autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for four hours. After natural cooling, the resulting powders underwent three cycles of washing with deionized water and ethanol alternately, followed by collection through centrifugation. The precipitates were then dried at 60 °C in an oven. Depending on the varied amounts of furfural added, the obtained samples were designated as 5-CDs@CdS NRs, 25-CDs@CdS NRs, 55-CDs@CdS NRs, and 85-CDs@CdS NRs. Typically, the sample used for characterizations and photocatalytic tests was denoted as 25-CDs@CdS NRs (referred to as CDs@CdS NRs for clarity), unless otherwise specified.

In a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, furfural (1 mL) and deionized water (60 mL) underwent a hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 300 min. Following cooling to room temperature, the resulting suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate insoluble matter, and the filtrate was subjected to freeze-drying, yielding a solid and providing furfural-derived carbon dots (CDs).

Solar light-driven hydrogen evolution reactions were performed using a Labsolar-6A circulation system (Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd) (Figure S1). Typically, the catalyst CDs@CdS NRs (20 mg) and sacrificial agents Na2S (3.6 g) and Na2SO3 (1.9 g) were stirred and dispersed in 100 mL of pure water within a flat-top, close-lid quartz reaction reactor. A 300 W Xenon lamp served as the light source, and the temperature of the solution was maintained at 5 °C by circulating cooling water. Throughout the reaction, the generated hydrogen was monitored online using gas chromatography (Fuli Instruments GC9790 Ⅱ) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector, with Ar employed as the carrier gas.

Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical experiments were conducted using a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation. All tests were carried out in a three-electrode configuration, employing saturated Ag/AgCl electrodes and Pt wire as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The potentials discussed below are referenced to the Ag/AgCl electrode. Deaeration was achieved through vacuuming. For the preparation of the working electrode, 4 mg of the synthesized photocatalyst was dissolved in a mixture of 100 μL Nafion solution, 400 μL deionized water, and 400 μL ethanol. Subsequently, 100 μL of the suspension slurry was deposited on the Indium-Tin Oxide (ITO) within a 5 mm diameter and dried at 60 °C to form a catalyst film. The electrolyte utilized was a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution with a pH of 7. A 300 W Xenon lamp with a light intensity of 100 mW/cm2 was employed, controlling the irradiated area at 1 cm2. Photocurrent density was measured with a bias voltage of 0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl under intermittently visible light irradiation. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted with a frequency ranging from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz in an aqueous Na2SO4 solution (0.5 M) without light. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 and a potential range of –1.2 V to 0.8 V.

CdS NRs were synthesized using a solvent-thermal method, exhibiting a smooth surface with a diameter of approximately 40 nm and lengths extending up to micrometers (Figures S2 and S3). Furfural, a sustainable natural material, was chosen as the precursor for carbon dots (CDs). The furfural-derived CDs, synthesized in a one-pot procedure, displayed sizes of 7 ± 2 nm (Figure S4) and were subsequently immobilized on the CdS NRs using a hydrothermal approach (Fig. 1
A). For comparative purposes, pristine CDs were prepared using the same method. Initial investigations involved the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CdS NRs, 25-CDs@CdS NRs, and CDs. The diffraction peaks corresponding to the hexagonal phase of wurtzite CdS (JCPDS No. 41–1049) were evident in both CdS NRs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs. However, characteristic diffraction peaks of CDs were not observed on 25-CDs@CdS NRs (Figure S5), likely due to their relatively low loading and high dispersion [40]. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images further illustrated the one-dimensional (1-D) nanorod morphology of 25-CDs@CdS NRs, confirming the successful loading of CDs onto the surface of CdS NRs (Fig. 1 B and C). A magnified TEM image (Fig. 1 D) revealed that the introduction of CDs did not alter the size of the CdS NRs and showed a uniform distribution of CDs (highlighted in red circles) on the surface (Figure S4). This observation was corroborated by the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of 25-CDs@CdS NRs (Fig. 1 E), where the resolved lattice fringes were carefully analyzed, as shown in Fig. 1 E and Fig. 1 F. The (100) plane of CdS and (102) plane of CDs were identified, with interplanar spacings of 0.36 and 0.18 nm, respectively. This close proximity between CDs and CdS NRs creates active sites assumed to enhance photocatalytic performance [46,47]. High-angle annular dark field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) and corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (Fig. 1 G-K) showcased the elemental distribution of Cd, S, C, and O, demonstrating the dispersion of CDs on the surface of CdS NRs. Raman spectra of 25-CDs@CdS NRs exhibited two distinct bands at ∼ 1340 and ∼ 1580 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands of CDs (Fig. 2
A and 2B) [48]. The D band is typically associated with a disordered sp2 hybridized carbon structure, while the G band signifies crystalline graphitization. In the Raman spectrum of carbon dots (CDs), the ID/IG ratio was calculated to be 0.99, suggesting a higher presence of graphitized structures within the CDs. Additionally, two peaks at 301 and 602 cm−1 corresponded to the longitudinal optical phonon mode of the CdS lattice and its overtone (Fig. 2 A) [49]. These Raman peaks once again confirmed the incorporation of graphitized CDs on the surface of CdS NRs.

Zeta potential analysis was employed to examine the charge characteristics of the materials. CdS NRs and furfural aqueous solution exhibited positive (ξ, +3.03 mV) and negative (ξ, −27.7 mV) zeta potentials, respectively (Fig. 2 C and 2D). This suggests that furfural could be electrostatically adsorbed onto the CdS NRs surface before carbonization. Furthermore, the zeta potential of the CDs aqueous suspension displayed high negative values (ζ, −37.5 mV), indicating the presence of electrostatic interaction between CDs and CdS NRs. Ultimately, a weak negative zeta potential ξ of − 2.35 mV was observed for CDs@CdS NRs, suggesting that the CDs adhered to the surface of CdS NRs (Fig. 2 C and 2D).

The robust interaction between CDs and CdS NRs was further substantiated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In the Cd 3d spectrum of 25-CDs@CdS NRs, typical peaks at 411.75 and 405.00 eV were observed, corresponding to Cd 3d3/2 and Cd 3d5/2 of Cd2+, respectively (Fig. 2 E) [50]. In the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2 F), peaks at 161.44 and 162.62 eV were assigned to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 of S2−, respectively [51]. The C1s spectrum of 25-CDs@CdS NRs (Fig. S6A) could be accurately deconvoluted into three peaks at 288.15 eV for CO, 286.15 eV for CO, and 288.15 eV for CC/CC [52]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum of 25-CDs@CdS NRs (Fig. S6B) exhibited peaks at 532.75 and 531.3 eV, corresponding to CO and CO, respectively, originating from CDs (Figure S7) [52]. These results unequivocally confirmed the successful loading of CDs on CdS NRs. In comparison with CdS NRs, the binding energy of Cd in 25-CDs@CdS NRs was positively shifted (∼0.95 eV), and a similar phenomenon was observed for S 2p (∼0.27 eV) (Fig. 2 E and 2F). These findings indicate that a portion of electrons was redistributed from CdS NRs to CDs due to the intense electrostatic interaction between the two components [53,54]. The intimate CDs/CdS NRs interface played a crucial role in facilitating this interaction. Additionally, the specific surface area is a critical catalyst parameter. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and BET-specific surface area of CdS and 25-CDs@CdS NRs are depicted in Figure S8. The minimal disparity in surface area and the comparable Type IV isotherms suggest that the CDs modification has no effect on the pore morphology of CdS NRs.

The as-prepared carbon dots (CDs), CdS NRs, and CDs@CdS NRs were subsequently assessed for visible-light-driven photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reactions in an aqueous solution (100 mL) containing Na2S (0.5 mol/L) and Na2SO3 (0.5 mol/L) as sacrificial agents. The time courses of H2 production and evolution rates are depicted in Fig. 3
A and B. Pristine CdS NRs exhibited a modest photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 298.82 μmol g−1h−1, while CDs showed no H2 production. In comparison, CDs@CdS NRs demonstrated a significantly improved photocatalytic performance, attributed to the increased absorbed photon flux, enhanced separation of photo-generated carriers, and accelerated electron transfer rate, collectively enhancing the hydrogen production rate. The H2 production rate in CDs@CdS NRs initially increased and then decreased with the rise in CDs loading, reaching the highest H2 production rate of 1075.76 μmol g−1h−1 in 25-CDs@CdS NRs. An optimal loading of CDs on the surface of CdS NRs facilitated charge transfer at the interface, thereby enhancing photocatalytic performance. Conversely, an excess of CDs may serve as recombination centers for electron-hole pairs [55].

Moreover, a physical mixture of CDs and CdS NRs in varying ratios (CDs/CdS NRs) exhibited a lower photocatalytic H2 production rate than their CDs@CdS NRs counterparts (Fig. 3 C and D). This outcome underscores the significance of an intimate electrostatic interaction at the CDs/CdS NRs interface in achieving higher photoactivity. As a control experiment, photocatalysts prepared by mixing different ratios of furfural and CdS NRs displayed negligible H2 production (Fig. 3 E and Figure S9). Furfural alone did not undergo conversion to CDs, and furfural-derived CDs were unable to produce H2. Consequently, ordered carbon was loaded onto CdS nanorods (CdS NRs) as depicted in Fig. 2 A. This strategic integration effectively enhances charge separation, consequently augmenting photocatalytic activity. Demonstrating optimal photocatalytic activity, 25-CDs@CdS NRs exhibited good photostability and durability (Fig. 3 F), with only a slight decrease in the H2 evolution rate observed in the third cycling test. Characterization techniques, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), indicated minimal changes in the crystal structure, morphology, and structural composition of 25-CDs@CdS NRs after the cycling test (Figures S10, S11, and S12). These findings unequivocally establish the superior photocatalytic activity and stability of CDs@CdS NRs. Furthermore, when comparing the H2 evolution rate of 25-CDs@CdS NRs with recent studies involving biomass-derived CDs and CdS photocatalysts, it becomes evident that the observed enhancement in photocatalytic hydrogen production surpasses many previously reported analogs. This highlights the advantages of furfural-derived carbon dots and their unique electrostatic interaction with CdS, a pivotal factor in enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the composites (Table 1 ) [56–60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge transfer pathway and photocatalytic mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
To unravel the contribution of carbon dots (CDs) in enhancing the photoactivity of CdS NRs, we examined the optical properties of the samples. The UV–vis absorption spectra were investigated, and the Kubelka-Munk function values of CdS NRs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs were calculated accordingly (Fig. 4
A-C). The absorption edge of pristine CdS NRs was at ∼ 525 nm, allowing for the deduction of a band gap of ∼ 2.43 eV. In comparison, 25-CDs@CdS NRs exhibited a red shift in the absorption edge, resulting in a reduced band gap of ∼ 2.41 eV. Moreover, the presence of CDs enhanced visible-light absorption in the range of 520–800 nm, enabling the capture of more photons to facilitate photoelectron excitation. These alterations in UV–vis spectra are also vividly reflected in the change in color of the specimen from light yellow to deep yellow after CDs loading (inset in Fig. 4 A).

The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra are presented in Fig. 5
A. Under 320 nm photoexcitation, pristine CdS NRs exhibited a strong emission peak at 540 nm, stemming from the interband recombination of electrons and holes. The lower emission intensity of 25-CDs@CdS NRs compared to CdS NRs indicated an inhibited recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Time-resolved PL decay spectra confirmed the recombination efficiency of photoinduced electrons and holes in 25-CDs@CdS NRs. The enhanced average fluorescence lifetime of 25-CDs@CdS NRs (0.67 ns) compared to that of pristine CdS NRs (0.64 ns) (Fig. 5 B) suggests the effective transfer of photoexcited electrons from CdS to CDs. This phenomenon has been supported by other reported studies [22,46,61]. This process mitigates charge carriers' recombination between CDs and CdS NRs, contributing to the prolonged electron-hole pair lifetime. The slower exponential decay kinetics observed in 25-CDs@CdS NRs play a crucial role in suppressing recombination, ultimately leading to an enhancement in photocatalytic activity. We investigated the photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), as shown in Fig. 5 C and Figure S13, respectively. As expected, the samples produced a current upon illumination with light and virtually no current when the illumination was removed. Fig. 5 C illustrates the photocurrent responses of both the bare CDs and 25-CDs@CdS NRs. The bare CDs exhibited minimal photocurrent response, while the photocurrent density of 25-CDs@CdS NRs was notably higher, approximately five times that of CdS NRs. Notably, the 25-CDs@CdS NRs exhibited a distinctive spike in the photocurrent signal upon illumination, in contrast to the CdS NRs, which displayed a more gradual increase. This distinctive behavior suggests that the integration of CDs within the CdS NRs has a beneficial effect on enhancing their electrical conductivity, as well as improving charge separation and transfer efficiency. Consequently, charge carriers generated within the top layer of the hybrid would reach the underlying Indium-Tin Oxide (ITO) more quickly, facilitating the transfer of electrons to the external circuit [62]. It is worth mentioning that the photocurrent of 25-CDs@CdS NRs decreased noticeably with the duration of illumination time, indicating a relatively larger absolute number of electron-hole pairs being recombined at the heterostructure's surface when charge density increases. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra were investigated to evaluate the electron transport capacity. Generally, the high-frequency zone reflects the charge transfer limiting process, which is related to the charge transfer resistance at the contacting interface with double-layer capacitance. Moreover, reduced charge-transfer resistance shows a smaller radius in a linear direction, reflecting accelerated charge transfer at the electrode/electrolyte interfaces. As shown in Figure S13, the radius of the 25-CDs@CdS NRs was smaller than CdS NRs, indicating that 25-CDs@CdS NRs possessed lower interfacial charge transfer resistance, which boosted the fast charge transfer at the interface.

Next, the linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) spectra of the samples were examined. Among them, 25-CDs@CdS NRs displayed the smallest onset potential at the same current density, confirming enhanced proton reduction kinetics (Fig. 5 D). The Tafel slope serves as an indicator of the efficiency of hydrogen evolution on the electrode surface, reflecting the ease with which hydrogen precipitation occurs. A smaller Tafel slope corresponds to a faster increase in current density, indicating a more facile hydrogen precipitation process. Analyzing the Tafel slopes (Figure S14) as shown in Fig. 5 D, it was evident that the Tafel slope value for 25-CDs@CdS NRs is the smallest among the samples. This observation suggested that 25-CDs@CdS NRs exhibit enhanced kinetics for the hydrogen evolution reaction. The smaller Tafel slope implied a more efficient electrocatalytic process for hydrogen evolution, signifying the favorable reaction kinetics of 25-CDs@CdS NRs. This characteristic, as evidenced by the Tafel slope analysis, underscores the promising potential of 25-CDs@CdS NRs in the realm of photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The nuanced understanding of reaction kinetics, as provided by the Tafel slope analysis, further supports the assertion of the superior catalytic activity of 25-CDs@CdS NRs in promoting hydrogen evolution under photoelectrochemical conditions. To obtain a quantitative picture of the overall photocatalytic efficiency, incident photo-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements of 25-CDs@CdS NRs, CdS NRs, and CDs were performed at various wavelengths of the incident photons and constant potential (+0.5 V). 25-CDs@CdS NRs showed higher IPCE values than those of CdS NRs and CDs across the entire wavelength range. The IPCE value increased from 0.27 % for CdS NRs to 0.44 % for 25-CDs@CdS NRs at 440 nm (Fig. 5 E), verifying enhanced solar absorption. In summary, benefiting from CDs loading, 25-CDs@CdS NRs demonstrated suppressed electron-hole recombination, enhanced photoresponse, and increased reaction kinetics, supporting its photocatalytic H2 production performance (Fig. 5 F). After completing all the electrochemical tests, we further investigated the structural stability of the samples through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis. The SEM results reveal the robustness and stability of the sample morphology, even after rigorous electrocatalytic testing, as shown in Figure S15. It is noteworthy that the surface nanoparticles are primarily attributed to the accumulation of sodium salts in the electrolyte. This confirms that the structural integrity of the catalyst is maintained, which is a crucial aspect of its long-term performance.

Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were subsequently employed to elucidate the mechanism behind the experimental results. The computational model was constructed by integrating a triangular CDs with a 4*4 supercell of the CdS (001) surface (Fig. 6 A). The selection of the triangular CDs was based on its reported synthetic feasibility [63]. To facilitate a clear comparison, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performance of the pristine CdS (001) surface was initially assessed by calculating the adsorption-free energy of protons at different sites (i.e., S and Cd). The adsorption energy of H+ serves as a well-established criterion for evaluating the HER activity, with an optimal catalyst exhibiting a value of 0 eV [64].

In our calculations, we observed that the pristine CdS (001) facet exhibited inertness towards the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The S site bound the proton too strongly, while the Cd site displayed extremely weak adsorption ability for the proton (Figure S16), resulting in inadequate adsorption strength at both sites. However, the introduction of CDs onto the CdS (001) plane induced slight distortions in both the CdS surface and CDs, leading to the activation of multiple HER-active sites around the pre-adsorbed CDs, as depicted in Fig. 6 B. Among the various potential sites labeled as S1 to S9, several sites demonstrated comparable or even superior adsorption energies compared to the commercial Pt|C catalyst (-0.09 eV). Specifically, S4 and S6 sites exhibited optimized proton adsorption-free energies closer to zero than Pt|C, while three sites (S1, S8, and S9) showed similar activity to Pt|C. The HER performance on the remaining three sites (S2, S3, and S7) was also significantly improved compared to the original S sites on the pristine CdS (001) plane (see Fig. 6 C). This enhancement could be attributed to the interaction between CDs and CdS NRs, as evidenced by the charge density difference plot, which revealed a substantial charge transfer of 0.74 electrons from CdS to CDs (Fig. 6 D). It is noteworthy that proton adsorption predominantly occurred at the S-top site in our calculations, and when H+ was pre-adsorbed on other sites (Figure S17), it relocated to the S-top site after structural relaxation. Additionally, as presented in Figure S18 in the supporting information, Pt|C was incorporated into the experimental setup to synthesize Pt|C@CdS NRs. The results indicate that the hydrogen evolution performance of 25-CDs@CdS NRs surpasses that of Pt|C@CdS NRs. These computational insights further support the notion that the appropriate interaction between CDs and the CdS substrate plays a crucial role in charge redistribution and the enhancement of HER kinetics.

Based on the experimental and characterization results presented above, we propose a reaction mechanism for the photocatalytic H2 evolution over CDs@CdS NRs, as depicted in Fig. 5 F. The incorporation of CDs in CDs@CdS NRs plays a pivotal role in reducing the bandgap and enhancing visible light absorption energetically. Upon photoexcitation, electrons are efficiently excited from CdS NRs, transitioning from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), thereby leaving holes in the VB of CdS NRs. While some electrons relax and return to the VB, others migrate to the CDs through the interface, facilitating the reduction of protons to H2. Simultaneously, the photogenerated holes in the VB of CdS are effectively trapped by the sacrificial agents (Na2S and Na2SO3). The establishment of electrostatic adsorption interfaces in CDs@CdS NRs significantly promotes transfer kinetics, driving the spatial separation of electron-hole pairs.

In conclusion, our work has successfully introduced a novel composite material by combining furfural-derived carbon dots (CDs) and CdS nanorods (CdS NRs) through a facile electrostatic interaction strategy. Particularly, the resulting composite, designated as 25-CDs@CdS NRs, has exhibited high photocatalytic efficiency in hydrogen (H2) production under visible light irradiation, achieving a rate of 1076 μmol g−1h−1. This rate represents a significant advancement, being approximately five times higher than the H2 production rate of pristine CdS NRs, which was limited to 299 μmol g−1h−1. The heightened H2 evolution efficiency in 25-CDs@CdS NRs is attributed to the established electrostatic interaction between CDs and CdS NRs. This interaction promotes efficient photocarrier transfer and spatial separation of electron-hole pairs. Additionally, the integration of CDs within the composite reduces the bandgap energy, resulting in enhanced visible light absorption and the activation of active sites for proton adsorption and reduction. It is noteworthy that previous literature suggests that the preparation methods for CDs involve calcination and secondary carbonization [40,45], and the integration between CDs and semiconductor materials typically relies on physically weak interface interactions. This work addresses this knowledge gap and provides valuable guidance for facile design of CDs-composite semiconductor materials.
